https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON – Jackson police are asking for the public’s help in identifying a woman whose skeletal remains were discovered earlier this year in the city.

The remains were found March 7, 2025, near Greenwood Avenue in Jackson, according to the Jackson Police Department. Investigators later sent the remains to the Mississippi Crime Lab in Pearl for examination.

Authorities said the remains were determined to belong to a female believed to be white, though investigators said Hispanic ancestry could not be ruled out.

The woman is believed to have been between 26 and 46 years old and approximately 63.5 to 71.3 inches tall, according to the crime lab’s findings.

Police said the woman had tattoos, and images of those tattoos have been released in hopes someone may recognize them and help identify her. One of them may say, “10-18-20.” The other one is a hand-drawn heart with “July” written in cursive above it. Inside the heart is “2018.”

The case remains under investigation.

Anyone with information that could help identify the woman is asked to contact Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477) or the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1234.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON – Jackson police are asking for the public’s help in identifying a woman whose skeletal remains were discovered earlier this year in the city.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Jackson police work to identify mostly skeletal remains through tattoo - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
GREENVILLE – The Greenville Police Department is investigating an assault that happened early Sunday morning outside a business on Highway 82 East.

Police said officers responded March 8 to the 1300 block of Highway 82 East, at Rack & Cue, after receiving a report of an assault.

When officers arrived, they spoke with a sheriff’s deputy who said the male victim had been escorted from the location earlier and was assaulted a short time later.

According to police, the victim said he was attacked in the parking lot by an unknown male after leaving the club.

The victim was taken to Delta Health for initial treatment and was later airlifted to another medical facility for further care.

Witnesses described the suspect as a Black male wearing a black shirt, blue jeans and a dark-colored sweater or hoodie.

Chief Marcus Turner said a video of the incident is circulating on social media and investigators are asking anyone who may recognize the suspect to help identify and locate the person of interest.

Police also said there were witnesses at the scene who have not yet come forward.

Anyone with information is asked to contact Crime Stoppers at 662-378-TIPS (8477) or submit a tip through the P3 app.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: GREENVILLE – The Greenville Police Department is investigating an assault that happened early Sunday morning outside a business on Highway 82 East.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Man airlifted after assault outside Greenville club - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
LEAKESVILLE – Several incarcerated men at the South Mississippi Correctional Institution recently graduated from the Compassion Prison Project’s Trauma Talks program.

The graduation marked the completion of a 16-part curriculum aimed at addressing some of the underlying issues that can contribute to incarceration.

Program participants studied the effects of adverse childhood experiences, also known as ACEs, and learned tools to help regulate their nervous systems, challenge self-limiting beliefs and build empathy for themselves and others.

The Trauma Talks curriculum was founded by Fritzi Horstman and uses educational videos, interactive workbooks and peer-led group discussions to encourage accountability and vulnerability among participants.

Officials said the program offers an important opportunity for men at SMCI, which houses nearly 3,000 inmates, to break cycles of violence and prepare for successful reentry into their communities.

“When people realize their inherent value, they don’t just transform themselves; they create a ripple effect that makes the entire prison environment safer,” a Compassion Prison Project representative said.

The graduation is the latest example of the Mississippi Department of Corrections’ stated focus on rehabilitation and reentry efforts.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: LEAKESVILLE – Several incarcerated men at the South Mississippi Correctional Institution recently graduated from the Compassion Prison Project’s Trauma Talks program.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Men at SMCI graduate from trauma recovery program - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
BOLIVAR COUNTY — More than two decades after she disappeared from a Bolivar County apartment complex, the remains of Stacy Hazelton have never been found, leaving her family without answers despite the conviction of the man accused of killing her.

Hazelton, a 21-year-old white female with brown hair and brown eyes, was last seen May 21, 2004, at the Alligator Place Apartments in Bolivar County. She stood about 5 feet 1 inch tall and weighed approximately 150 pounds at the time of her disappearance.

Investigators say Hazelton had moved to Mississippi earlier that year with her boyfriend, James Fannings Jr., but remained in close contact with family members in Pennsylvania. The couple lived with Fannings’ longtime friend, Chevaun Mack.

According to investigators, the couple soon began struggling financially. Authorities later learned Hazelton had told family members she wanted to return home and asked her parents to send money so she could leave Mississippi. Her parents sent the money, but Hazelton never returned.

After months without hearing from their daughter, Hazelton’s parents reported her missing in December 2004.

The case took a major turn in January 2006 when Fannings was arrested and charged with Hazelton’s murder. Investigators said Mack told authorities he witnessed Fannings shoot Hazelton in the head during an argument inside the apartment.

According to Mack’s account, Fannings forced him to help dispose of Hazelton’s body. The men placed her remains inside a barrel and loaded it into the back of Hazelton’s Chevrolet Blazer. Mack told investigators Fannings drove around with the barrel in the vehicle for several days before attempting to burn the body. The remains were then allegedly buried in a remote area near a creek in Duncan, Mississippi.

Authorities say Fannings later drove Hazelton’s Blazer to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

Fannings went to trial later in 2006. Prosecutors relied heavily on Mack’s testimony, and much of the remaining evidence was circumstantial. After about 16 minutes of deliberation, a jury found Fannings guilty of murder. He was sentenced to life in prison.

Fannings appealed the conviction in 2008, but the courts upheld the verdict.

Despite the conviction, Hazelton’s remains have never been located.

Authorities continue to urge anyone with information about the location of Hazelton’s remains to contact investigators.

Anyone with information can call the Pennsylvania State Police at (717) 299-7650, referencing case number J01-1049751, or the Bolivar County Sheriff’s Department at (662) 843-5378.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: BOLIVAR COUNTY — More than two decades after she disappeared from a Bolivar County apartment complex, the remains of Stacy Hazelton have never been found, leaving her family without answers despite the conviction of the man accused of killing her.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: MISSING IN MISSISSIPPI: Stacy Hazelton, Bolivar County - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
OXFORD – An Oxford man has been arrested after police investigated a reported attempted sexual assault that happened in late February, according to the Oxford Police Department.

Police said the department received a walk-in report March 5 regarding an attempted sexual assault that happened the night of Feb. 26.

During the investigation, officers learned the victim had been in the downtown area before leaving and walking toward her home. While walking along University Avenue, she was approached by a driver who offered her a ride home, police said.

After entering the vehicle, the driver took her to another location, where he allegedly held her against her will and attempted to sexually assault her.

Through investigative leads developed by the victim and investigators, police identified the suspect as Keilun Treveon Lewis, 19, of Oxford.

Lewis was arrested and charged with attempted rape and kidnapping. Bond was set by a Municipal Court judge.

Police said the case remains under investigation.

The Oxford Police Department is encouraging residents and visitors to stay alert and aware of their surroundings, particularly when accepting rides. Officials urged people not to enter vehicles with individuals they do not know and to verify rideshare details through the app before getting in.

Police also encouraged downtown visitors to use designated rideshare pickup locations near the north end of the Square by the parking garage, the west side behind the Lyric and the east side near Harrison’s and South 14th Street. Officials said those areas are well-lit and monitored by cameras.

Anyone with additional information about the incident, or who may have experienced a similar incident, is asked to contact the Oxford Police Department at 662-232-2400.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: OXFORD – An Oxford man has been arrested after police investigated a reported attempted sexual assault that happened in late February, according to the Oxford Police Department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Oxford man charged after attempted sexual assault, kidnapping investigation - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
TUNICA COUNTY — The Tunica County Sheriff’s Office is investigating a series of shootings that left at least six people wounded over two days in Tunica County and led to multiple arrests following a high-speed chase into DeSoto County.

Deputies first responded around 11:58 p.m. Saturday, March 7, after a 911 caller reported he had been shot in the area of Fitzgerald Boulevard in Robinsonville, according to the sheriff’s office. When deputies and first responders arrived, they found Jaylin Liddell, 18, of Tunica, suffering from a gunshot wound. Liddell was treated at the scene by Pafford Emergency Medical Services and transported to an area hospital.

Several hours later, at 3:08 a.m. Sunday, March 8, deputies were dispatched to the Tunica River Park at 1 Riverpark Drive after callers reported multiple people had been shot. At the scene, deputies located Recovyon Reed, 21, of Tunica, who had been shot. Reed was treated at the scene and transported to a hospital for treatment.

Authorities continued receiving reports of gunfire throughout the early morning hours. At 4:23 a.m., deputies responded to a report of shots fired in the parking lot of the Waffle House at 13132 U.S. Highway 61 in Robinsonville, but no victims were located.

Less than 30 minutes later, at 4:52 a.m., deputies were notified by a hospital that Deonte Taylor, 20, of Tunica, had arrived by personal vehicle seeking treatment for a gunshot wound.

At 5:18 a.m., deputies received another report of shots fired on Prichard Road in the Old Sub area of Tunica. Deputies in the area spotted a black Infiniti leaving the scene at a high rate of speed and attempted a traffic stop.

The vehicle failed to stop and led deputies on a high-speed chase into DeSoto County, traveling along Arkabutla Dam Road toward Arkabutla Lake Dam. The vehicle eventually crashed, and three occupants fled on foot. With assistance from the DeSoto County Sheriff’s Office, deputies pursued and apprehended the suspects.

Authorities identified the three individuals as Antonio Allen, 23, of Tunica; Mylon Done, 22, of Friars Point; and Darrius Robinson, 31, of Memphis. All three were taken into custody. During an inventory of the vehicle, deputies said they recovered multiple firearms in plain view.

At 5:23 a.m., deputies received another 911 call reporting a shooting at a residence on Gay Street in the Old Sub area of Tunica. Deputies found Ceedrick Wheeler, 21, of Tunica, suffering from a gunshot wound. Wheeler was treated at the scene and transported to a hospital.

Later, at 6:03 a.m., deputies were notified by another hospital that Khritavious Magsby, 30, of Clarksdale, had arrived seeking treatment for a gunshot wound.

Authorities said an additional suspect, Jon Talton “JT” Carter, 22, of Tunica, has also been taken into custody. Carter is charged with attempted murder in connection with the shooting that occurred at Tunica River Park.

The violence continued into the following day. At 1:16 p.m. Monday, March 9, deputies responded to reports of shots fired on Curtistine Drive in Tunica. Deputies found Mario Lester, 18, of Tunica, suffering from a gunshot wound. He was treated at the scene and transported to a hospital.

Investigators identified Charles Davis Jr., 19, of Tunica, as a person of interest in that shooting. Davis turned himself in to the Tunica County Sheriff’s Office at about 3:15 p.m. Monday and remained in custody as the investigation continued.

Sheriff’s officials said the investigation remains active and could lead to additional arrests. Authorities believe the shootings may be connected to gang activity and retaliatory violence.

The Tunica County Sheriff’s Office is being assisted by the Tunica Police Department, DeSoto County Sheriff’s Office, Mississippi Bureau of Investigation and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives.

Anyone with information about the shootings is asked to contact the Tunica County Sheriff’s Office at (662) 363-1411 or Crime Stoppers at (662) 910-0400.

The following individuals are currently out on bond pending ongoing criminal investigations, according to the sheriff’s office:

• Jaylin Liddell – granted $50,000 bond for second-degree murder charge • Deonte Taylor – granted $25,000 bond for three capital murder charges, four counts of conspiracy, four counts of shooting into a motor vehicle, and one count of drive-by shooting • Ceedrick Wheeler – granted $25,000 bond for aggravated assault charge • Mario Lester – granted $100,000 bond for murder charge • Antonio Allen – granted $35,000 bond for conspiracy and accessory after the fact
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: TUNICA COUNTY — The Tunica County Sheriff’s Office is investigating a series of shootings that left at least six people wounded over two days in Tunica County and led to multiple arrests following a high-speed chase into DeSoto County.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Six Wounded in Series of Tunica County Shootings; Multiple Suspects Arrested After High-Speed Chase - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON – Jackson police have arrested a suspect in connection with a fatal shooting that occurred early Monday morning.

According to the Jackson Police Department, homicide detectives identified 42-year-old Patrick Harris as the suspect in the shooting death of 27-year-old Anthony Watts.

Police said Watts was shot and killed earlier today in the 600 block of Northpointe Parkway.

Harris was arrested later that morning and charged with murder, aggravated assault and shooting into an occupied vehicle. He was booked into the Jackson Police Department holding facility and is awaiting his initial appearance in Municipal Court.

Jackson Police said the investigation remains ongoing.

Anyone with information about the case is asked to contact the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1234 or Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477).
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON – Jackson police have arrested a suspect in connection with a fatal shooting that occurred early Monday morning.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Suspect arrested in fatal shooting of 27-year-old in Jackson - Darkhorse Press

VICKSBURG – Three juveniles have been charged as adults in connection with a shooting that occurred March 5 in Vicksburg, according to the Vicksburg Police Department.

Police said the shooting happened at the intersection of Randolph Street and Locust Street. No injuries were reported.

Following an investigation, officers identified three juvenile suspects who were taken into custody. The suspects appeared in court March 6, where they were adjudicated and charged as adults.

Authorities identified the suspects as Zion Pierce, 16, and Brian Bell, 16, both charged with aggravated assault. Each received a $600,000 bond.

Kaiden Knight, 15, was charged with aggravated assault and shooting into a motor vehicle. His bond was set at $550,000.

Police said the investigation remains active and additional information will be released as it becomes available.

Anyone with information about the incident is asked to contact the Vicksburg Police Department or submit an anonymous tip through Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477).
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: VICKSBURG – Three juveniles have been charged as adults in connection with a shooting that occurred March 5 in Vicksburg, according to the Vicksburg Police Department.

PEARL – Law enforcement officials have made another arrest as part of an ongoing narcotics investigation in Rankin County, according to the Rankin County Sheriff’s Department.

On March 9, investigators with the Rankin County Sheriff’s Department and the Pearl Police Department, along with agents from multiple federal agencies, executed a search warrant and an arrest warrant at a home on Bruin Avenue in Pearl.

Authorities said the search warrant was obtained following an extensive investigation into drug activity in the area.

Steven Richard Hoffland was identified during the investigation as allegedly receiving large quantities of narcotics and selling the drugs.

Hoffland was arrested and charged with possession of a controlled substance, trafficking a controlled substance and sale of a controlled substance.

He was booked into the Rankin County Jail, and his bond has been set at $900,000.

Officials said Hoffland will appear before Rankin County Court Judge David Morrow for his initial court appearance.

Investigators said the broader investigation into drug trafficking in the metro area is ongoing and additional arrests are expected.

Rankin County Sheriff Bryan Bailey thanked Pearl Police Chief Nick McClendon, officers with the Pearl Police Department and other agencies involved for their cooperation in the investigation.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PEARL – Law enforcement officials have made another arrest as part of an ongoing narcotics investigation in Rankin County, according to the Rankin County Sheriff’s Department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Pearl man arrested in ongoing Rankin County narcotics investigation - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
GREENWOOD – Bank of Commerce is proud to announce a total donation of $250,000 to six organizations across North Mississippi through the Mississippi Children’s Promise Act, supporting programs that provide care, resources, and critical support for children and families throughout the region.

The Children’s Promise Act, passed by the Mississippi Legislature in 2019, provides a dollar-for-dollar tax credit to individuals and companies that donate to qualified charitable organizations serving children in need.

Through this initiative, Bank of Commerce provided support to the following organizations:

“These organizations provide essential services and compassionate care for children and families across our communities,” said Bryan Thornhill, President and CEO of Bank of Commerce. “We are honored to support their work and invest in organizations that make a meaningful difference in the lives of Mississippi families.”

“At Bank of Commerce, our goal has always been to support the places where we live and work,” said Samantha Pigott, Vice President of Deposit & Treasury Solutions at Bank of Commerce. “We are proud to stand alongside these organizations and the important work they do every day.”

The donation will help these organizations continue providing critical services including family support programs, counseling, housing assistance, and resources that strengthen children and families across the region.

Bank of Commerce has a long-standing commitment to investing in the communities it serves. Through initiatives like the Children’s Promise Act, the bank continues to partner with local organizations to help strengthen Mississippi families and build a stronger future for the region.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: GREENWOOD – Bank of Commerce is proud to announce a total donation of $250,000 to six organizations across North Mississippi through the Mississippi Children’s Promise Act, supporting programs that provide care, resources, and critical support for children and families throughout the region.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Bank of Commerce Donates $250,000 to Six North Mississippi Organizations Through Mississippi Children’s Promise Act - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
BILOXI – Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced the sentencing of a Biloxi man on a child sexual abuse material charge following a joint investigation by state and federal authorities.

Terrance Allan Green, 46, of Biloxi, was sentenced on one count of possession of child sexual abuse material, according to the attorney general’s office.

The case was investigated by the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office and the Federal Bureau of Investigation, with assistance from the Biloxi Police Department. It was prosecuted by the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the Southern District of Mississippi.

“Predators who seek to exploit children in Mississippi will not go unpunished, and my office is committed to holding these individuals accountable,” Fitch said in a statement. “I am grateful to our partners at the FBI and the U.S. Attorney’s Office for their work to secure this sentence. Together, we are making Mississippi a safer place for everyone.”

On March 6, U.S. District Court Chief Judge Sul Ozerden sentenced Green to 10 years in the custody of the Federal Bureau of Prisons, followed by lifetime supervised release.

Green was also ordered to pay $15,000 in restitution and $3,000 in fees. Officials said he is already registered as a sex offender.

According to the attorney general’s office, Green’s arrest and sentencing stemmed from the same 2025 undercover operation initiated by the office that also led to the sentencing of Steven Dessino last month.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: BILOXI – Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced the sentencing of a Biloxi man on a child sexual abuse material charge following a joint investigation by state and federal authorities.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Biloxi man sentenced to 10 years in federal prison for child sexual abuse material charge - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
LEE COUNTY – A female child was found dead inside a vehicle Tuesday morning after it crashed off the Natchez Trace Parkway and into a creek in Lee County, according to WTVA.

The Lee County coroner confirmed the child’s death. First responders were also searching for another missing person, WTVA reported.

According to Tupelo Fire Chief Thomas Walker, the crash happened around 8:30 p.m. Monday during heavy rain and flash flooding in the area.

Authorities said a witness saw the vehicle leave the parkway and enter the creek, then immediately called 911.

Police and fire crews searched the area for hours but were initially unable to locate the vehicle. Investigators later used a drone to search the area and found the car in the creek between Palmetto Road and Graham Drive.

Officers located the female child inside the vehicle. She was pronounced dead at the scene.

WTVA reported that members of Mississippi Task Force 1 from Monroe and Lee counties, the Tupelo Fire Department, Tupelo Police Department, Lee County Sheriff’s Office and Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries and Parks were involved in the search for the other missing person.

Officials have not yet released the child’s identity. The crash remains under investigation.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: LEE COUNTY – A female child was found dead inside a vehicle Tuesday morning after it crashed off the Natchez Trace Parkway and into a creek in Lee County, according to WTVA.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Child found dead after vehicle crashes into creek off Natchez Trace Parkway - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
QUITMAN — Clarkco State Park in Quitman will reopen March 19 following renovations that include updated RV campsites, newly renovated cabins and improvements to other park facilities, officials with the Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries and Parks announced.

The park will celebrate its grand reopening with a public event beginning at 8 a.m. March 19 with a social gathering at Pavilion Two, followed by a special program. Guests will also have the opportunity to tour the park’s newly renovated cabins and campground after the program.

Clarkco State Park now includes 43 updated campsites designed for RV camping, each equipped with full hookups including water, electricity and sewer, along with a picnic table and grill, according to MDWFP.

Officials say the upgrades are intended to make the park a more comfortable destination for visitors looking to enjoy outdoor recreation in Mississippi.

The park also features six newly renovated cabins, each outfitted with updated amenities including stainless steel kitchen appliances such as a refrigerator, stove, microwave and coffee station. The cabins also include renovated dining areas with solid-surface countertops, new living room furniture with a flatscreen satellite television, updated bedroom furniture and bathrooms with walk-in fiberglass showers.

Each cabin also features new outdoor spaces including a front porch with a picnic table, fire pit and grill, as well as a screened-in back porch with outdoor seating.

Additional renovations at the park include updates to the lodge and bathhouse, according to the department.

Reservations for cabins and campsites are now open and can be made online through the Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries and Parks reservation system.

Visitors to Clarkco State Park can also take part in a variety of recreational activities including fishing, boating and hiking, along with access to a day-use area featuring a playground, nature trail, disc golf course and tennis courts.

Park entry costs $2 per person, and children age 5 and younger are admitted free.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: QUITMAN — Clarkco State Park in Quitman will reopen March 19 following renovations that include updated RV campsites, newly renovated cabins and improvements to other park facilities, officials with the Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries and Parks announced.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Clarkco State Park to Reopen March 19 With Renovated Cabins, Campgrounds - Darkhorse Press

LUCEDALE – A Lucedale man has been arrested on multiple charges of touching a child for lustful purposes, according to the George County Sheriff’s Office.

Investigators arrested Matthew Bradshaw, 43, on March 9.

Bradshaw was charged with three counts of touching a child for lustful purposes. Authorities also said he had two outstanding warrants through George County Justice Court for guilty in absentia.

The charges stem from an investigation that began after the sheriff’s office received a report from Child Protection Services. Investigators later developed information that led to Bradshaw’s arrest, officials said.

Bradshaw was given a $60,000 commercial bond on each of the three child exploitation charges, for a total bond of $180,000.

Sheriff Mitchell Mixon said the sheriff’s office appreciates the work of Child Protection Services in cases involving the safety and well-being of children.

All defendants are presumed innocent until proven guilty in a court of law.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: LUCEDALE – A Lucedale man has been arrested on multiple charges of touching a child for lustful purposes, according to the George County Sheriff’s Office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: George County man arrested on child exploitation charges - Darkhorse Press

QUITMAN COUNTY – Twenty-five participants at the Quitman Community Work Center recently completed forklift training through a partnership between the Mississippi Department of Corrections and Coahoma Community College.

The training program concluded Friday, March 6, and provided participants with hands-on instruction designed to prepare them for employment opportunities after reentry into the community.

Officials said the certification gives participants industry-recognized skills while also helping build confidence and readiness to enter the workforce.

The Mississippi Department of Corrections said programs like the forklift training are part of broader efforts to reduce recidivism and create pathways to meaningful employment for individuals preparing to return to society.

MDOC officials also credited community partnerships, including Coahoma Community College, for helping provide training opportunities that support rehabilitation and workforce development.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: QUITMAN COUNTY – Twenty-five participants at the Quitman Community Work Center recently completed forklift training through a partnership between the Mississippi Department of Corrections and Coahoma Community College.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Inmates at Quitman Community Work Center earn forklift certification - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
CLEVELAND — A Lake man was arrested following a monthlong investigation and charged with enticement of a child to produce sexually explicit material and human trafficking, according to the Cleveland Police Department.

Bailey Jones, 30, was arrested last week and charged with one count of enticement of a child to depict sexually explicit material and one count of human trafficking, police said.

Authorities also executed a search warrant at Jones’ home during the investigation with assistance from multiple state and local agencies.

Jones was transported to Bolivar County, where he appeared before Cleveland Municipal Court Judge Warren Bell for his initial court appearance. Bell set Jones’ bond at $750,000 on each charge, according to police.

Jones is currently being held at the Bolivar County Jail.

The search warrant and investigation involved assistance from several agencies, including the Mississippi Bureau of Investigation Special Victims Unit, the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office, the Mississippi Department of Public Safety Crime Scene Unit, the Mississippi Highway Patrol, the Mississippi Department of Corrections K-9 Unit and the Scott County Sheriff’s Department.

Cleveland Police Chief Dudley Tribble thanked investigators and the assisting agencies for their work on the case.

Authorities noted that Jones is presumed innocent unless proven guilty in court.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: CLEVELAND — A Lake man was arrested following a monthlong investigation and charged with enticement of a child to produce sexually explicit material and human trafficking, according to the Cleveland Police Department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Lake Man Charged With Child Enticement, Human Trafficking After Cleveland Investigation - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
GREENE COUNTY – A Lucedale man was killed Monday afternoon in a motorcycle crash in Greene County, according to the Mississippi Highway Patrol.

The crash happened March 9 at approximately 3:56 p.m. on U.S. Highway 63 near Pleasant Hill Road.

Authorities said a 2011 Harley-Davidson motorcycle driven by Ross M. Eubanks, 56, of Lucedale, was traveling south on U.S. 63 when it left the roadway and struck the tree line.

Eubanks sustained fatal injuries in the crash, officials said.

The Mississippi Highway Patrol is continuing to investigate.

TUNICA COUNTY – Authorities have filed additional charges and say more arrests are expected following a shooting investigation in Tunica County that left six people wounded.

According to an update released by the Tunica County Sheriff’s Office, several suspects appeared in Tunica County Justice Court on March 10 for initial appearances on charges connected to the shooting.

Darius Robinson was given an $81,000 bond on charges of attempted murder, drive-by shooting and unlawful discharge of a firearm within a populated area.

Antonio Allen, 23, of Tunica, also received an $81,000 bond on charges of attempted murder, drive-by shooting and unlawful discharge of a firearm within a populated area.

Mylon Done was given a $101,000 bond on charges of attempted murder, drive-by shooting, unlawful discharge of a firearm within a populated area and felony fleeing.

Jon Talton “JT” Carter was denied bond on an attempted murder charge.

Authorities said Allen later posted bond and was released from the Tunica County Sheriff’s Office on March 10 at about 6:45 p.m.

About 45 minutes later, deputies with the DeSoto County Sheriff’s Office made contact with a vehicle near the Arkabutla Lake Dam in DeSoto County. As a result of that encounter, Allen and others were taken into custody.

Allen was then charged in DeSoto County with tampering with physical evidence and conspiracy.

Investigators allege the individuals conspired to move, hide and transport physical evidence connected to the Tunica County shooting investigation.

Officials said that part of the case is now being investigated by the DeSoto County Sheriff’s Office and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives.

The Tunica County Sheriff’s Office said the broader investigation remains active and ongoing. Authorities said additional arrests and charges are possible, including charges related to attempted murder, drive-by shooting and possible gang retaliation.

The sheriff’s office is being assisted by the Tunica Police Department, DeSoto County Sheriff’s Office, Mississippi Bureau of Investigation and ATF.

Anyone with information is asked to contact the Tunica County Sheriff’s Office at 662-363-1411 or Crime Stoppers at 662-910-0400.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: TUNICA COUNTY – Authorities have filed additional charges and say more arrests are expected following a shooting investigation in Tunica County that left six people wounded.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Additional charges filed after Tunica shooting that left six wounded - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
GREENWOOD – Bank of Commerce is proud to announce the approval of a $274,000 loan to the City of Itta Bena to support critical water and sewer system improvements. The loan will provide matching funds required for the city’s Mississippi Community Water Infrastructure (MCWI) Grant.

The funding will assist in modernizing and improving the city’s existing water and sewer infrastructure, helping ensure reliable service for residents and businesses while addressing aging system components identified in the city’s engineering report.

“This project represents an important investment in the health, safety, and future of Itta Bena,” said Timothy Stanciel, Senior Vice President and CRA Officer at Bank of Commerce. “Strong infrastructure is essential to economic development and quality of life. We are proud to partner with the City to help make these improvements possible.”

“Supporting projects that strengthen local infrastructure is an important part of our commitment to the communities we serve,” said Bryan Thornhill, President and CEO of Bank of Commerce. “We are proud to partner with the City of Itta Bena on improvements that will benefit residents today and support future growth.”

The City of Itta Bena formally authorized the loan through an adopted resolution. Mayor Mary Tyes-Williams is serving as the City’s representative for the project.

“Reliable water and sewer systems are vital to our community,” said Mayor Mary Tyes-Williams. “This partnership with Bank of Commerce allows us to move forward with much-needed upgrades that will benefit our residents today and support growth in the years ahead.”

As a community bank founded in 1904, Bank of Commerce remains committed to supporting local municipalities, businesses, and families across Mississippi. Through relationship-driven banking and community-focused lending, the bank continues to invest in projects that strengthen infrastructure and build long-term prosperity.

At Bank of Commerce, caring for our communities means helping create opportunities for growth and long-term stability. This investment in Itta Bena.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: GREENWOOD – Bank of Commerce is proud to announce the approval of a $274,000 loan to the City of Itta Bena to support critical water and sewer system improvements. The loan will provide matching funds required for the city’s Mississippi Community Water Infrastructure (MCWI) Grant.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Bank of Commerce Approves $274K Loan for Itta Bena Water, Sewer Improvements - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
(StatePoint) About 20 years ago, chickens—long a staple of farms and homesteads—began showing up in urban and suburban yards. They enabled city dwellers to source homegrown food, provided a touch of nature amid the urban jungle, and have continued to rise in popularity ever since. According to the American Pet Products Association’s 2025 State of the Industry report, 11 million U.S. households own backyard chickens, a 28% increase from 2023.

Caroline Komar-Steiner, a Team Lead at the Tractor Supply store in Antioch, Illinois, is a passionate chicken expert who regularly guides individuals into the world of chicken ownership. She and her husband have been raising poultry since 2018, and she describes it as one of the most rewarding things she has ever done. “Raising those chicks and getting your first eggs is an ‘eggsperience’ unlike any other,” she said.

To ensure new chick owners can enjoy that experience, Komar-Steiner hosts regular Chickens 101 sessions at the store. The first thing she always tells people is to find out if their town or neighborhood allows chickens. Some have restrictions, so it’s best to find that out on the front end. Then she asks them why they want chickens. Is it for their eggs? For meat? Will they be purely ornamental? “That steers me toward what they’re looking for,” Komar-Steiner said.

To help you get started, Komar-Steiner is sharing these basics for preparing for and caring for chickens:

1. Assess your space: Chicks need a temperature-controlled space until they are fully feathered, usually about six weeks. Komar-Steiner recommends finding a warm, draft-free area. She sets her brooder up in her basement. The chicks grow quickly, so it’s important to determine where you’ll put your coop and have it ready to go. The chickens will also need a heat source in winter to stay warm and prevent their water from freezing.

2. Get supplies, then chicks: It’s a good idea to buy all your equipment and supplies before bringing chickens home. You’ll need a brooder, shavings, heat lamps or brooder plates, a perch so they can learn how to roost, a feeder and a waterer. Komar-Steiner recommends adding an electrolyte to the chicks’ water to give them an extra edge. You’ll also need chick grit, feed and a thermometer.

3. Remember, they are living things: People often get excited about the idea of a steady supply of eggs, but like all living things, chickens require care. Before leaving home for an extended time, make sure someone is available to check on their food and water, especially in winter and summer. And like other animals, chickens are at risk of getting sick. Tractor Supply carries a wide variety of products to support chicken health.

Even after having chickens for a time, customers still frequently have questions, especially when it comes to adding to their flock, which leads Komar-Steiner to a critical point. “It is imperative that you do not put baby chicks in with adult birds,” she said.

Chickens view new animals as either food or intruders. Komar-Steiner’s recommendation is to wait until they are large enough to hold their own, usually around three months. Additionally, introducing new adult birds requires an integration process.

With 30 chickens and five turkeys of her own, Caroline is an excellent resource for poultry advice, especially during Tractor Supply’s annual Chick Days event. Customers can purchase baby birds in store and enjoy new products, new breeds and special offers until September 13. The products and insight to keep chickens happy and healthy are available year round at Tractor Supply and TractorSupply.com.

Before you build a backyard flock, get the equipment and know-how you need for successful chicken ownership.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: (StatePoint) About 20 years ago, chickens—long a staple of farms and homesteads—began showing up in urban and suburban yards. They enabled city dwellers to source homegrown food, provided a touch of nature amid the urban jungle, and have continued to rise in popularity ever since. According to the American Pet Products Association’s 2025 State of the Industry report, 11 million U.S. households own backyard chickens, a 28% increase from 2023.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Chick Check: What to Know Before Building a Backyard Chicken Flock - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON — A recent fentanyl strike force operation in Lee and Prentiss counties resulted in multiple criminal charges and six immigration detainers, Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced.

The joint operation involved the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office, the North Mississippi Narcotics Task Force, Homeland Security Investigations, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement and the Mississippi Alcoholic Beverage Control.

Authorities said the operation resulted in two felony charges, six misdemeanor charges and six ICE detainers.

Fitch said the operation marks the first enforcement action completed by the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office under the federal 287(g) Task Force program, which allows certain state and local law enforcement agencies to partner with ICE on immigration enforcement.

“Our office is proud to participate in the 287(g) program to make Mississippi safer than ever before,” Fitch said in a statement. “We are grateful to work alongside such dedicated and skilled federal, state and local law enforcement partners. Together, we are taking dangerous criminals off the streets and restoring peace and security in our communities.”

Deputy Field Office Director Brian Acuna of the ICE New Orleans Field Office said the program is intended to enhance public safety by targeting criminal activity linked to illegal immigration.

“This is exactly what the ICE 287(g) program is all about — keeping the public safe from the threat of fentanyl and removing criminal illegal aliens from our communities,” Acuna said.

Under Section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, state and local law enforcement agencies can receive federal training and authority to help identify individuals who may be subject to immigration enforcement while in local custody.

According to the Attorney General’s Office, the agency was the first in Mississippi to participate in the program by training and certifying all of its sworn law enforcement officers as members of the 287(g) Task Force.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — A recent fentanyl strike force operation in Lee and Prentiss counties resulted in multiple criminal charges and six immigration detainers, Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Fentanyl Strike Force Operation in North Mississippi Results in ICE Detainers, Charges - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
PEARL — A GoFundMe fundraiser has been launched to help the owner of The Edible Paper Store & Co. in Pearl recover after a fire damaged the specialty baking supply shop and forced it to close indefinitely.

The store, located at 2901 Old Brandon Road, specializes in edible images, custom cake toppers and decorations for cakes, cupcakes and other baked goods. Owner Kat Reynolds said the fire damaged the building’s electrical system and wiring, requiring extensive repairs before the business can reopen.

According to a description on the fundraiser page, Reynolds runs much of the business herself, from designing custom edible images and toppers to managing daily operations. The fire destroyed or damaged several essential items she relies on to produce her products, prompting supporters to organize the online fundraiser to help replace equipment and supplies.

“Rebuilding is crucial not only for her business but also for the community,” the GoFundMe description states. “Every donation, big or small, helps her recover and continue to provide the sweet treats that bring joy to so many.”

Reynolds said in a social media update that while the storefront will remain closed during repairs, she plans to continue limited operations from home so customers can still order edible image printing and cake toppers.

“It could have been much worse,” Reynolds wrote, noting that most of the store’s inventory remained intact despite the fire.

Customers needing supplies are encouraged to contact the store by email or phone, and staff members will retrieve items from the shop once or twice daily while cleanup and repairs continue.

The business is also searching for a potential new location in the Pearl area while the current building undergoes repairs.

The Edible Paper Store & Co. is typically open six days a week and sells baking supplies ranging from food coloring and fondant to silicone molds, boxes and other decorating tools for home and professional bakers.

In her statement, Reynolds thanked the Pearl Fire Department, emergency medical personnel and the Pearl Police Department for their quick response to the fire.

“We are incredibly grateful for the work you do for our community,” she wrote.

Organizers say the fundraiser will help Reynolds replace critical equipment and continue serving local bakers as the shop works to recover.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PEARL — A GoFundMe fundraiser has been launched to help the owner of The Edible Paper Store & Co. in Pearl recover after a fire damaged the specialty baking supply shop and forced it to close indefinitely.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Fire Damages Pearl's Edible Paper Store - Darkhorse Press

PEARL – The Pearl Police Department recovered about $6,500 in stolen merchandise following an investigation into a retail theft ring targeting businesses in Pearl.

Officers first responded Feb. 6 at about 1 p.m. to a reported shoplifting at Ulta Beauty. By the time officers arrived, the suspects had left with merchandise valued at about $3,150, police said.

The department’s Criminal Investigations Division opened an investigation and identified two suspects as Aureka Ducksworth and Keanna Gordon. Investigators said a third person involved had not yet been identified at that time. Officers also identified a suspect vehicle by reviewing surveillance footage.

On March 8 at about 6 p.m., officers with the Patrol Division, Special Operations Division and Criminal Investigations Division received information that the group was again targeting retail stores in Pearl. Officers later found the suspect vehicle at the Outlets of Mississippi.

A search of the vehicle led to the recovery of about $6,500 in stolen merchandise believed to be tied to multiple retail theft cases, police said.

Authorities charged Ducksworth with felony shoplifting, conspiracy and placed a hold on her for a felony shoplifting warrant from another jurisdiction.

Keanna Gordon was also charged with felony shoplifting, conspiracy and held on a felony shoplifting warrant from another jurisdiction.

Police said Sharnique Gordon, who was not pictured in the department’s release, was charged with felony shoplifting, conspiracy and held on a felony shoplifting warrant from another jurisdiction.

Investigators said they are working with multiple jurisdictions on returning the recovered merchandise and continuing the prosecution of the suspects.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PEARL – The Pearl Police Department recovered about $6,500 in stolen merchandise following an investigation into a retail theft ring targeting businesses in Pearl.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Pearl police recover $6,500 in stolen merchandise in retail theft investigation - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — Mississippi law enforcement leaders say changes to the state’s public employee retirement system have put first responders at a disadvantage and are worsening recruitment and retention challenges across the state.

Andy “Ski” Matuszewski, Mississippi division president of the Southern States Police Benevolent Association, said a task force of first responder representatives formed in July 2025 following the passage of House Bill 1 during that year’s legislative session. The measure made significant changes to the Public Employees’ Retirement System, or PERS, including the Tier 5 retirement structure for newer employees placing first responders in a 35 year plan with less benefits than under the previous retirement tier.

Matuszewski said the group, made up largely of presidents and representatives of public safety organizations, began meeting to develop a response to the legislation and advocate for Tier 5 reforms in the face of evidence of a growing disconnect between legislators and the men and women who protect and serve Mississippians.

“We felt we had no choice other than to organize a response,” Matuszewski said, noting that a study conducted by the Stennis Institute supported concerns that Mississippi’s new Tier 5 retirement benefits for first responders now lag behind neighboring states.

He said the group’s position is that public safety careers should be treated differently than other state jobs because of the physical and mental demands of the work. Higher cancer rates, heart disease, and more suicides for first responders also point to reasons why their retirement should be different, in addition to the immediate dangers of the job.

“Expecting a first responder to work for 35 years for a fraction of what the people they are working beside daily receive is not only unreasonable but patently unfair,” Matuszewski said.

Members of the task force met with lawmakers and officials several times over the past year, including a January meeting with staff from PERS and meetings with legislators supporting proposals to modify the system.

Matuszewski said the group also sought meetings with Lt. Gov. Delbert Hosemann, whose office oversees the Senate. In addition the SSPBA has supported Hosemann in the past, believing him to have first responders’ best interests in mind.

During one meeting in February at the Capitol, Matuszewski said members of the task force met with Hosemann, his staff, Sen. Daniel Sparks, who authored legislation related to Tier 5 retirement, and PERS Executive Director Ray Higgins.

According to Matuszewski and others in the meeting, the meeting began with the lieutenant governor skipping all ceremony such as handshaking and standing in front of his desk and asking the group flat out what they wanted, stating that he had to get back to the Senate floor. As the discussion continued, Sparks began explaining details of proposed legislation addressing retirement concerns.

Matuszewski and others said Hosemann interrupted Sparks during one part of the explanation and suggested the first responders present would not understand the technical details being discussed.

“He interrupted him as Sparks started to go into some detail on a particular nuance, saying words to the effect of ‘these guys won’t understand that,'” Matuszewski said.

Matuszewski said the comment was frustrating for members of the task force, which includes experienced law enforcement officers, firefighters and other public safety professionals.

The retirement debate has been a major issue during the current legislative session. On March 6, House Speaker Jason White announced that the House had amended Senate Bill 2103 by replacing its language with provisions addressing some concerns raised by first responder groups.

Matuszewski said first responders across Mississippi are closely watching the outcome.

He said the state is already struggling to recruit and retain police officers, firefighters, emergency medical personnel, corrections officers and 911 telecommunicators, and warned that failing to address future retirement benefits could push experienced workers to leave for neighboring states offering better compensation and incentives.

“If public safety doesn’t work, nothing else matters,” Matuszewski said.

The SSPBA president also noted that the first responders fighting for the Tier 5 specifications to be fair have nothing to gain from it. The whole point is future generations, not only of first responders, but of those they will be protecting.

The pushback also follows comments late last year by Lt. Gov. Delbert Hosemann, who said first responders “live longer than most people do because they stay healthy.”

Medical and occupational research has repeatedly shown that police officers and firefighters face elevated health risks that can shorten careers and lives. A long-term mortality study of police officers published in Policing: An International Journal found officers experienced a higher risk of early death compared with the general population, particularly during the first years of service.

Mental health is also a significant concern among public safety workers. Studies show that about one in four first responders experience depression, a statistic that reflects only reported and diagnosed cases.

Suicide has also emerged as a leading cause of death among first responders. The Ruderman Family Foundation reported that more police officers and firefighters died by suicide in 2017 than were killed in the line of duty, citing higher rates of depression and post-traumatic stress disorder tied to repeated exposure to traumatic incidents. Nationally, more officers die from suicide than from line-of-duty deaths in many years.

Firefighters also face increased cancer risks due to repeated exposure to hazardous materials. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention says firefighters are frequently exposed to carcinogens such as benzene, formaldehyde and asbestos during emergency responses. The agency created the National Firefighter Registry for Cancer to study those risks, while the International Association of Fire Fighters reports firefighters are more likely than the general population to develop several types of cancer.

Research also shows first responders are exposed to far more traumatic events than most civilians. The Law Enforcement Alliance for Peer Support cites research indicating first responders encounter an average of 178 critical incidents over the course of their careers, compared with two to three traumatic events experienced by the average civilian.

Occupational stress has also been linked to higher rates of family strain among public safety workers. Research published by the University of Maryland School of Public Health found that irregular schedules, cumulative trauma and shift work contribute to increased marital instability among police officers and other first responders.

Public safety organizations across Mississippi have also weighed in on the debate surrounding Senate Bill 2103 and proposed retirement changes.

In a March 6 Facebook statement, the Mississippi Association of Chiefs of Police said it appreciates the House’s efforts to work with first responders as lawmakers consider changes to the retirement system.

“We appreciate the House working with first responders and recognizing that recruitment and retention are critical for Mississippi’s public safety community,” the organization said in a Facebook statement. “First responders face extraordinary physical and mental health demands while protecting our communities, and many are looking for the stability needed to continue serving safely and effectively. We look forward to continuing to work with the Senate as discussions move forward to support those who protect Mississippi every day.”

The organization also said the House amendment maintains eight-year vesting for first responders and does not include a back-to-work provision.

The Mississippi Deputy Sheriffs Association has also urged lawmakers to continue considering the legislation, warning that allowing the bill to die could send the wrong message to public safety workers and educators across the state.

“The men and women who serve as deputies, police officers, firefighters, EMS personnel, and teachers across Mississippi do not ask for much,” the association said in a social media statement. “They simply ask for the ability to do their jobs, protect their communities, and provide for their families with dignity and security.”

The group emphasized that Senate Bill 2103 represents more than a political debate.

“SB 2103 is not a political talking point. It is about the future of public safety and education in our state,” the Mississippi Deputy Sheriff’s Association posted.

The organization also urged lawmakers to give the House amendment full consideration and called on first responders and citizens to contact senators and the lieutenant governor’s office about the issue.

“We are hearing concerning rumors that Senate leadership may allow this bill to die without proper consideration,” the Mississippi Deputy Sheriff’s Association said, encouraging first responders to attend the First Responders Caucus at the State Capitol on March 12.

“When we stand united, our voices are harder to ignore,” the association said.

Matuszewski said first responder organizations are increasingly paying attention to how lawmakers approach public safety issues and retirement policy.

He said groups representing thousands of public safety workers are closely tracking legislative votes and will likely play a larger role in evaluating candidates in future elections.

“First responders show up for the people we protect and serve each and every day,” Matuszewski said. “We have the right to expect our elected officials to do the same for us.”

An email seeking comment from Hosemann’s office was not answered by press time.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — Mississippi law enforcement leaders say changes to the state’s public employee retirement system have put first responders at a disadvantage and are worsening recruitment and retention challenges across the state.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: 'They Won't Understand That': First Responders Say Lt. Gov. Dismissed Their Retirement Concern - Darkhorse Press

YAZOO COUNTY — A woman was taken into custody early Monday after deputies say she fled from law enforcement and struck two patrol vehicles on the grounds of a correctional facility in Yazoo County.

The incident began around 3 a.m. when deputies encountered a white vehicle whose driver had reportedly called a tow truck driver in Madison County to change a tire. After the tow truck driver installed the tire, the driver of the vehicle allegedly left the scene without paying for the service.

The tow truck driver later contacted Yazoo County dispatch after the vehicle crossed into Yazoo County, authorities said.

Deputies quickly located the vehicle and attempted a traffic stop, but the driver fled, leading deputies on a pursuit.

During the chase, the driver turned onto the grounds of a correctional facility in Yazoo County, where authorities say she struck two patrol vehicles while continuing to try to evade deputies.

The collisions eventually disabled the suspect’s vehicle, and deputies took the driver into custody at the scene.

Authorities said neither of the deputies involved was injured, and the driver was also not injured.

Additional details, including the driver’s identity and potential charges, were not immediately released.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: YAZOO COUNTY — A woman was taken into custody early Monday after deputies say she fled from law enforcement and struck two patrol vehicles on the grounds of a correctional facility in Yazoo County.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Woman Arrested After Pursuit, Collisions With Patrol Vehicles in Yazoo County - Darkhorse Press

YAZOO COUNTY — Two men were arrested following a traffic stop on Highway 49, according to the Yazoo County Sheriff’s Department.

Authorities said Deputy Wilkins noticed a vehicle traveling northbound without a tag and initiated a traffic stop.

Two men were inside the vehicle, and both were taken into custody without incident.

No additional information was immediately released by the sheriff’s department.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.content_html:
TUPELO – A Tupelo man has been arrested on an aggravated trafficking of cocaine charge following a traffic stop and search warrant, according to the North Mississippi Narcotics Unit.

Authorities said agents made a traffic stop March 5 on a black Ford F-150 on South Eason Boulevard in Tupelo. The driver was identified as Anthony Dixon.

During the stop, agents developed probable cause to search the vehicle, which resulted in the discovery of a felony amount of suspected cocaine and marijuana.

Investigators later executed a search warrant at Dixon’s home on Pierce Street in Tupelo. During that search, agents said they found more than 1 1/2 pounds of suspected cocaine, 1 pound of suspected marijuana and more than $175,000 in cash.

Anthony Christopher Dixon, 46, of Tupelo, was arrested and charged with aggravated trafficking of cocaine.

Officials said Dixon was ordered held without bond by Tupelo Municipal Court Judge Willie Allen.

The investigation remains ongoing, and additional charges may be pending.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.summary: TUPELO – A Tupelo man has been arrested on an aggravated trafficking of cocaine charge following a traffic stop and search warrant, according to the North Mississippi Narcotics Unit.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.title: Tupelo man charged with aggravated trafficking of cocaine after drug investigation - Darkhorse Press

WAVELAND — Police in Waveland are asking for the public’s help identifying a person caught on camera who investigators say is connected to several vehicle burglaries and the theft of a vehicle earlier this month.

According to the Waveland Police Department, the incidents occurred Tuesday, March 3, 2026, when the suspect allegedly broke into multiple vehicles and stole a vehicle from a Ford dealership in Waveland.

Authorities released surveillance images of the individual and are asking anyone who recognizes the person to come forward.

Police said anyone with information about the suspect or the crimes is urged to contact Crime Stoppers by downloading the P3 Tips app or by calling **TIPS.

Officials noted that in order to qualify for any potential reward, information must be submitted through Crime Stoppers.

The investigation remains ongoing.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.summary: WAVELAND — Police in Waveland are asking for the public’s help identifying a person caught on camera who investigators say is connected to several vehicle burglaries and the theft of a vehicle earlier this month.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.title: Waveland Police Seek Help Identifying Suspect in Vehicle Burglaries, Stolen Car - Darkhorse Press

CORINTH — The Corinth Police Department is asking for the public’s help locating a missing man.

Police said Neal Jalufka, 45, has been reported missing from Corinth.

Authorities said his last known location and clothing description are unknown.

Anyone who sees Jalufka or has information about his whereabouts is asked to contact the Corinth Police Department at 662-286-3377 and request Detective Jerry Rogers.
